nativeRange.habitat: The Black Edge Moray Eel is most commonly found in seagrass beds and shallow coastal banks, and is occasionally encountered in bays. Unlike many moray species, it is not commonly associated with coral reefs. It is a bottom-dwelling species recorded at depths of roughly 10–19 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on fish, squid, octopus, and crustaceans. Newly acquired specimens have been known to initially prefer live feeder fish; with patience, many individuals can be transitioned to frozen meaty foods such as silversides, squid, and krill. Target feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is generally advisable to avoid accidental hand bites.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth

imageCandidates.notes: NOAA Photo Library, 2100x1340. Live in-situ individual crawling on a sandy/shell bottom (matching this species' seagrass/sand habitat), sharp and well-lit; clearly shows the ocellated white-spot pattern with dark dorsal saddle blotches and dark-and-pale banded tail. Only live-specimen photo available under a commercial-friendly license.

summary: The Black Edge Moray Eel (Gymnothorax saxicola) is a Western Atlantic species native to seagrass beds and coastal banks rather than coral reefs. It tends to be secretive and nocturnal, and is generally considered an intermediate-level fish that requires ample rockwork for shelter and a tightly sealed lid to prevent escape.
careNotes:
The Black Edge Moray Eel (also known as Honeycomb Moray or Ocellated Moray) is native to the Western Atlantic, ranging from New Jersey and Bermuda south to southern Florida and the eastern Gulf of Mexico, with records from Cuba and Belize. In the wild, it is most commonly associated with seagrass beds and shallow coastal banks (10–19 m depth) and is generally not found on coral reefs, though it adapts well to reef aquarium rockwork in captivity.

Escape risk: Like all moray eels, this species tends to be a determined escape artist with a surprisingly powerful body. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is considered essential — any gap large enough for the eel's body is a potential exit point.

Nocturnal behavior: This species tends to be crepuscular to nocturnal. Newly acquired specimens typically remain hidden in rock crevices with their mouth visibly open (a normal behavior, not aggression). Over time, many individuals become bolder and begin displaying openly during the day.

Feeding: Newly acquired individuals may initially prefer live feeder fish, but patient transition to frozen meaty foods (squid, silversides, krill) is generally achievable. Target feeding with tongs reduces the risk of accidental hand bites. This eel has been documented to eat fish and invertebrates that fit in its mouth — ornamental shrimp and small tankmates are at risk.

Reef compatibility: This species tends to leave corals and sessile invertebrates undisturbed, but will readily consume fish and mobile invertebrates, including shrimp and crabs, that fit in its mouth.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (60 cm TL, consistent with LiveAquaria's 24 in / 60.96 cm), native range (Western Atlantic, New Jersey to Gulf of Mexico, Cuba, Belize), habitat (seagrass beds, not coral reefs), taxonomy (Muraenidae / Anguilliformes), and temperature (23.4–28°C — wider range than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F). Temperature values averaged per playbook. FishBase common name is 'Honeycomb moray'; 'Black Edge Moray' appears to be a LiveAquaria trade name. FishBase does not list 'Black Edge Moray' in its common-names registry.

fish.breedingNotes: In the wild, this species reproduces by broadcast spawning, releasing eggs into the water column to develop externally. Captive breeding has not been documented.
